questions.hint: The metric evolves opposite to the Ricci curvature.
questions.explain: The flow is $\partial_t g_{ij}=-2R_{ij}$. The minus sign tries to round out positive curvature and stretch out negative curvature; the factor of 2 matches the Bianchi identity normalisation that makes the equation parabolic.

questions.q: On an Einstein metric $\mathrm{Ric}=\lambda g$, the Ricci flow starting from $g_0$ is given by:
questions.explain: If $g(t)=\rho(t)g_0$ then $R_{ij}(g(t))=R_{ij}(g_0)=\lambda g_0{}_{ij}$ (Ricci is invariant under constant rescaling). So $\rho'(t)=-2\lambda$, giving $\rho(t)=1-2\lambda t$. Spheres ($\lambda>0$) shrink to a point at $t=1/(2\lambda)$; hyperbolic metrics ($\lambda<0$) expand.

questions.q: Why is Ricci flow described as a 'heat equation for the metric'?
choices: It dissipates energy by the heat-kernel formula.
In harmonic coordinates the principal part is $\partial_t g_{ij}=\Delta g_{ij}+\text{lower order}$.
It is identical to the heat equation $u_t=\Delta u$.
It satisfies the maximum principle for $g$ itself.
questions.hint: Look at the linearisation in a good coordinate gauge.
questions.explain: In harmonic coordinates $-2R_{ij}=\Delta g_{ij}+Q(g,\partial g)$ where $Q$ collects nonlinear lower-order terms. So Ricci flow is a quasilinear parabolic system whose principal symbol is the rough Laplacian — that is the precise sense in which it is a heat equation for $g$.

questions.q: Hamilton's short-time existence theorem says that for any smooth initial metric $g_0$ on a closed manifold:
choices: the Ricci flow exists for all time $t\ge 0$
a smooth solution exists on a maximal interval $[0,T)$ with $T>0$, and is unique
a solution exists only if $g_0$ has positive Ricci curvature
no smooth solution exists in general
questions.hint: Compactness and parabolic-system theory give existence + uniqueness up to a maximal time.
questions.explain: On a closed manifold there is a unique smooth solution on some $[0,T)$ with $T>0$, and $T$ is maximal: either $T=\infty$ or curvature blows up as $t\to T$. The proof requires repairing parabolicity via DeTurck.

questions.q: Why is the bare Ricci flow equation NOT strictly parabolic?
choices: Its symbol has a positive eigenvalue.
Diffeomorphism invariance produces a kernel in the principal symbol along gauge directions.
$R_{ij}$ is a rank-2 tensor, so the equation has too many components.
It is third order, not second order.
questions.hint: If $\phi_t$ is a 1-parameter family of diffeomorphisms, $\phi_t^* g(t)$ also solves Ricci flow.
questions.explain: Because Ricci flow is invariant under pull-back by diffeomorphisms, infinitesimal gauge changes $\mathcal{L}_X g$ lie in the kernel of the linearised principal symbol. So the system is only weakly parabolic; strict parabolicity (and standard PDE theory) fails until the gauge is fixed.

choices: adding $\mathcal{L}_W g$ for a vector field $W$ chosen so the modified flow is strictly parabolic, then pulling back by $\phi_t^{-1}$ to recover Ricci flow
switching to harmonic coordinates everywhere on $M$
questions.hint: The added term is a Lie derivative chosen to cancel the gauge degeneracy.
questions.explain: DeTurck adds $\mathcal{L}_W g$ where $W^k = g^{ij}(\Gamma^k_{ij}-\tilde\Gamma^k_{ij})$ relative to a fixed background metric. The modified flow is strictly parabolic and has a unique solution by standard theory; pulling back along the diffeomorphisms generated by $-W$ recovers an honest Ricci flow.

questions.q: If a Ricci flow on a closed manifold reaches its maximal time $T<\infty$, what must happen?
questions.hint: Smooth solutions extend past $T$ unless a quantity blows up.
questions.explain: Hamilton's continuation criterion: a smooth Ricci flow on a closed manifold extends past $T$ unless $\sup |\mathrm{Rm}|\to\infty$ as $t\to T$. Curvature blow-up is the only obstruction to long-time existence.

questions.q: A Type I singularity is one where, near the singular time $T$:
questions.hint: Type I = the natural parabolic blow-up rate.
questions.explain: $|\mathrm{Rm}|$ scales like $1/\text{(distance to singular time)}$ under parabolic rescaling. Type I means this rate is achieved as an upper bound; Type II is supercritical (e.g. degenerate neckpinches); Type III is for immortal flows.

questions.q: Parabolic rescaling about a singular point produces, in the limit, a solution defined for all $t\in(-\infty,0]$. Such a solution is called:
questions.hint: It exists arbitrarily far back in time.
questions.explain: An ancient solution is a Ricci flow defined on $(-\infty,T_0]$. Hamilton and Perelman showed that blow-up limits at Type I/II singularities are $\kappa$-noncollapsed ancient solutions, and tangent flows of these encode the local geometry of the singularity.

questions.q: A Ricci soliton is a metric $g_0$ together with a vector field $X$ and constant $\lambda$ such that:
questions.hint: The metric should evolve only by scaling and pull-back along $X$'s flow.
questions.explain: The soliton equation $\mathrm{Ric}+\tfrac12\mathcal{L}_X g=\lambda g$ encodes self-similarity: $g(t)=\sigma(t)\phi_t^* g_0$ with $\sigma(t)=1-2\lambda t$ and $\phi_t$ the flow of $X/\sigma(t)$ solves Ricci flow. Einstein metrics are the special case $X=0$.

questions.q: A soliton is called shrinking, steady, or expanding according to the sign of $\lambda$. Which is which?
choices: the sign of $\lambda$ does not determine the type
questions.explain: $\lambda>0$ gives $\sigma(t)$ decreasing — the metric shrinks (and goes singular at $t=1/(2\lambda)$); $\lambda=0$ gives $\sigma\equiv 1$ — translating soliton; $\lambda<0$ gives $\sigma$ increasing — the metric expands.

questions.q: Which of the following is a steady Ricci soliton?
questions.hint: Steady = $\lambda=0$, gradient soliton on a non-compact surface.
questions.explain: Hamilton's cigar $g=\frac{dx^2+dy^2}{1+x^2+y^2}$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$ is the canonical 2-D steady gradient soliton. The round sphere is shrinking ($\lambda>0$, Einstein); hyperbolic space is expanding ($\lambda<0$); flat $\mathbb{R}^n$ is static (also a degenerate soliton).

questions.q: An $\epsilon$-neck in a 3-D Ricci flow is a region of the manifold which, after rescaling so that the curvature is order 1, is $\epsilon$-close in $C^k$ to:
questions.hint: Necks look long and thin — one dimension is stretched.
questions.explain: An $\epsilon$-neck is a region modelled on $S^{n-1}\times[-\epsilon^{-1},\epsilon^{-1}]$ (in 3-D: $S^2\times\mathbb{R}$) after parabolic rescaling. Singular regions in 3-D Ricci flow develop $\epsilon$-necks generically.

choices: cutting the neck along a 2-sphere cross-section and gluing in two standard caps
removing the entire connected component containing the neck
letting the flow evolve through the singularity unmodified
questions.hint: Topologically: cut $S^2\times\mathbb{R}$ at a slice, cap off both pieces with $D^3$.
questions.explain: Cut along a cross-sectional $S^2$ inside the neck and glue a standard cap (a $D^3$ with a controlled metric) onto each side. The resulting manifold may have more components, but each surgery is a simple topological operation: removal of a $S^2\times I$ and attachment of two 3-balls.

questions.q: On any finite time interval, Hamilton–Perelman surgery requires:
choices: only finitely many surgeries, and they have no accumulation point
exactly one surgery per singular time
infinitely many surgeries that accumulate at every singular time
questions.hint: Volume bounds and noncollapsing limit how often surgery can fire.
questions.explain: Perelman's noncollapsing and curvature estimates ensure that surgery scales stay bounded below on each finite interval and that volume drops by a fixed amount per surgery, so surgery times are isolated and finite-in-number on any $[0,T]$.

questions.q: Perelman's proof of the Poincaré conjecture shows that on any closed simply connected 3-manifold $M$, Ricci flow with surgery:
choices: exists for all time without surgery
becomes extinct (the manifold disappears) in finite time
produces a flat metric in the limit
questions.hint: Extinction means $M$ becomes empty after finitely many surgeries.
questions.explain: Perelman proved that Ricci flow with surgery on a closed simply connected 3-manifold becomes extinct in finite time. The path of surgeries shows $M$ is a connected sum of finitely many $S^3$'s and $S^2\times S^1$'s; simple connectivity forces $M\cong S^3$, proving Poincaré.

questions.q: Two of Perelman's monotone quantities along Ricci flow are:
choices: the Euler characteristic and the genus
the first Betti number and the signature
questions.hint: Both are functionals on metrics introduced specifically for Ricci flow.
questions.explain: The $\mathcal{F}$-functional is monotone along Ricci flow (steady-soliton energy); the $\mathcal{W}$-entropy is monotone along the flow combined with rescaling and gives the noncollapsing theorem. The reduced volume is the geometric counterpart.

questions.q: Thurston's geometrisation conjecture, proved by Perelman, says every closed orientable 3-manifold decomposes (along spheres and tori) into pieces each carrying one of:
choices: either spherical or hyperbolic geometry only
questions.explain: Thurston's eight model geometries are the maximal simply connected homogeneous 3-spaces with cocompact isometry group. Geometrisation says every prime 3-manifold is built from pieces locally isometric to one of these; Ricci flow with surgery realises the decomposition geometrically.
